title: VaR and Its Drawbacks

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-06-finance.md

setup.narrative: VaR_alpha = the alpha-percentile loss: P(loss > VaR_alpha) = 1 - alpha. We test it on TWO independent CDS positions, each with default prob 3%, $1M loss given default.
rules: Two independent CDSs: P(>=1 default) = 1 - (0.97)^2 ~ 5.91%. So VaR_95 > 0.
setup.question: Why is VaR not subadditive? Compute VaR for the joint vs sum-of-individual.

learningObjectives: Define VaR as a percentile of the loss distribution.
Demonstrate the sub-additivity violation with the two-CDS example.
Recognize CVaR (expected shortfall) as the coherent alternative.

stages.label: Single CDS VaR
stages.prompt: Short CDS on bond A: P(default) = 3%, loss = $1M | default, 0 otherwise. What is VaR_95?

options.text: VaR_95 = 0.  (Loss is 0 with probability 97% > 95%, so the 95th percentile loss is 0.)

stages.explanation: VaR_alpha is the smallest L such that P(loss > L) <= 1 - alpha. With P(loss = 0) = 0.97 and P(loss = $1M) = 0.03, the 95th percentile of the loss distribution is 0. So VaR_95(A) = 0 -- even though we have real credit risk!

stages.label: Joint VaR
stages.prompt: Now add an INDEPENDENT short CDS on bond B with the same parameters. P(at least one default) = 1 - (0.97)^2 = 0.0591 ~ 5.91% > 5%. What is VaR_95(A + B)?

options.text: VaR_95(A + B) = $1M.  (P(loss >= $1M) = 5.91% > 5%, so the 95th percentile crosses into the loss region.)

stages.explanation: P(loss = 0) = 0.97^2 = 0.9409, P(loss = $1M) = 2 * 0.03 * 0.97 = 0.0582, P(loss = $2M) = 0.03^2 = 0.0009. Cumulative: P(loss <= 0) = 0.9409 < 0.95, so the 95th percentile is in the next bucket -> VaR = $1M.

stages.label: Commit
stages.template: VaR_95(A) + VaR_95(B) = 0 + 0 = __[a]__.  VaR_95(A + B) = __[b]__.  Sub-additivity (VaR(A+B) <= VaR(A) + VaR(B)) is __[c]__ (satisfied / violated).  CVaR / Expected Shortfall is the __[d]__ (coherent / incoherent) alternative.
stages.explanation: Sub-additivity is violated: VaR(A+B) = $1M > 0 + 0 = VaR(A) + VaR(B). This contradicts the diversification intuition. CVaR (average loss given loss > VaR) IS coherent (sub-additive, monotone, translation-invariant, positively homogeneous).

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: How would VaR_99 behave for the SINGLE CDS? (P(loss = 1M) = 3% > 1%, so VaR_99 = $1M.)
Compute CVaR_95 for the joint position. (CVaR = E[loss | loss > VaR_95]. With VaR = $1M and tail prob 5.91%, tail expected loss > $1M.)
Why does Basel III mandate Expected Shortfall over VaR? (Because ES is coherent, captures tail risk, and aggregation works correctly.)
